summaries.Etymology: No relevant information regarding Etymology is available.
summaries.Geology: Nishinoshima is an andesitic volcanic island located at latitude 27°14'49" N and longitude 140°52'28" E, situated at the summit of a large submarine volcano approximately 30 km in diameter. The island measures approximately 650 m long (southwest to northeast) and approximately 200 m wide, with an area of approximately 77,000 m², an elevation of 25 m, and several reefs on its northeast side. The SiO2 content of ejecta ranges between 51.8 and 60.1 wt%, indicating a composition spanning basaltic andesite to andesite. The first eruption in recorded history occurred in May 1973, originating on the seafloor approximately 400 m east of the southern tip of the island, beginning as an undersea phreatomagmatic explosion and progressing semicircularly, with five craters ejecting volcanic blocks and lava flows. Successive new islands formed in September and October 1973, with diameters ranging from 30 to 150 m and heights up to 40 m, eventually connecting above sea level by October 10, 1973, and being named Nishinoshima Shinto on December 21, 1973. Each volcanic edifice experienced severe erosion, though removed deposits linked the old and new island, and the mouth of the bay became closed off by deposit movement in 1982. A further eruption in 1974 produced an additional new island at the northeastern tip of Nishinoshima, and a major series of eruptions in 2013 destroyed nearly all vegetation on the island.
summaries.Ecology: Nishinoshima Island, part of the Ogasawara Island chain approximately 600 miles south of Tokyo, serves as a significant ecological case study due to the impact of frequent volcanic eruptions on plant life, with eruptions nearly wiping out most plant life each time they occur. The 2019 eruption led to the extinction of the native purslane (Portulaca oleracea) on the island, a species otherwise found across tropical regions globally, prompting researchers from Tokyo Metropolitan University to extract specimens before the eruption for preservation. The Ogasawara Islands more broadly are noted for exceptionally high endemism rates due to flora and fauna having evolved independently from mainland Asia, and the islands provide ongoing examples of speciation and ecological succession in isolation. Nishinoshima, formed by submarine volcanic eruption in 1973, offers a real-time case study of island colonization and ecosystem development, with scientists conducting ongoing research on volcanic activity, species colonization, and marine ecosystem formation. Academic research on the ecology of the region includes studies on Ardisia dominant forest ecology, the influence of feral goats, regeneration of mesic forest following typhoon disturbance, endangered plant species and their causal factors, competitive relationships between native and introduced plant species, and the evaluation of human impact on the nature of the Ogasawara Islands.
